front: Earned Value basics — PV, EV, AC, BAC

legend.meaning: Budget at Completion — the total approved budget
Planned Value — budgeted cost of work scheduled by now
Earned Value — budgeted cost of work actually done
Actual Cost — what's really been spent so far

back:
Earned value compares three S-curves over time. Read them at 'time now':

- EV below PV → behind schedule
- AC above EV → over budget

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><line x1='200' y1='26' x2='200' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='200' y='176' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>time now</text><polyline points='40,160 90,150 140,118 200,92 258,52 292,38' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.6'/><polyline points='40,160 92,150 140,128 200,108' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2'/><polyline points='40,160 92,146 140,116 200,88' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='200' cy='92' r='3' fill='currentColor'/><circle cx='200' cy='108' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='200' cy='88' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><text x='296' y='34' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>PV</text><text x='150' y='104' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10'>AC</text><text x='150' y='140' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10'>EV</text></svg>
figure.caption: PV (planned), EV (earned), AC (actual). Here EV < PV (behind) and AC > EV (over budget).

legend.meaning: Fraction of work planned to be complete by now

back: The budgeted cost of the work scheduled to be done by a point in time — your baseline of where you planned to be.
The budgeted cost of the work actually completed. EV is the heart of EVM — every index and variance compares against it.
tip: EV uses the BUDGETED cost of completed work, not the actual money spent (that's AC).

back:
- Positive → under budget
- Negative → over budget
tip: Variances are EV minus something; indices are EV divided by something. Negative variance = bad.

back:
- Positive → ahead of schedule
- Negative → behind schedule

Value earned for every unit of cost spent — your cost efficiency.

- > 1.0 → under budget
- = 1.0 → on budget
- < 1.0 → over budget

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='30' y1='52' x2='290' y2='52' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='40' x2='160' y2='64' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='160' y='32' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>1.0</text><circle cx='40' cy='52' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='280' cy='52' r='3' fill='#2F6F5E'/><text x='44' y='82' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10'>‹ 1.0 · over budget</text><text x='276' y='82' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>› 1.0 · under budget</text></svg>
figure.caption: CPI above 1.0 is good (under budget); below 1.0 is over budget.

tip: Above 1.0 is good. Both use EV as the numerator: CPI = EV / AC and SPI = EV / PV.

back:
Rate of progress against plan — your schedule efficiency.

- > 1.0 → ahead of schedule
- = 1.0 → on schedule
- < 1.0 → behind schedule
Use when current cost performance is expected to continue for the rest of the project — the most common EAC on the exam.
Use when the current variance is a one-off and remaining work will go to plan — finish the rest at the budgeted rate.

legend.meaning: Estimate to Complete (a fresh bottom-up estimate)

back: Use when the original estimate is flawed and you re-forecast the remaining work from scratch.

front: Estimate at Completion (EAC) — cost and schedule
back: Use when both cost and schedule performance will affect the remaining work — the most pessimistic EAC.
The expected cost to finish the remaining work — what's left of the forecast after what you've already spent.

How much over or under the original budget you expect to finish.

- Positive → under budget
- Negative → over budget

The cost efficiency you must hit on the remaining work to still finish on budget.

- > 1.0 → you must perform better than before (harder)
- < 1.0 → you have slack
tip: Use (BAC − AC) in the denominator to hit the original budget; use (EAC − AC) to hit the new forecast.

back: A weighted average that leans heavily on the most likely value, giving a realistic expected duration or cost.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='30' y1='150' x2='300' y2='150' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><path d='M50,150 C95,150 110,46 138,46 C176,46 235,150 290,150' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='60' y2='138' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='138' y1='150' x2='138' y2='48' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='180' y1='150' x2='180' y2='96' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='280' y1='150' x2='280' y2='138' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='60' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>O</text><text x='138' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>M</text><text x='180' y='166' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>E</text><text x='280' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>P</text><text x='160' y='28' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>E = (O + 4M + P) / 6</text></svg>
figure.caption: The expected value E lies between Optimistic and Pessimistic, weighted toward Most Likely.

tip: The 4× weight on Most Likely is what makes PERT a weighted (beta) average.

back: A simple average of the three estimates — used when you don't want to weight the most likely value.
tip: Triangular = simple average (÷3). Beta/PERT = weighted average with 4× on M (÷6).

back: The spread of the estimate — a measure of its risk. A wider gap between optimistic and pessimistic means more uncertainty.

legend.meaning: Financial effect if it occurs (+ opportunity, − threat)

back:
The average outcome of a risk. Sum the EMV of every branch in a decision tree to compare options.

- Threats are negative, opportunities positive
tip: A risk with 20% probability and a $50k threat has an EMV of −$10k.

back: The number of two-way communication paths. It grows fast — adding people adds disproportionately more channels to manage.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 195' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='150' y1='30' x2='217' y2='78' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='150' y1='30' x2='191' y2='158' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='150' y1='30' x2='109' y2='158' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='150' y1='30' x2='83' y2='78' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='217' y1='78' x2='191' y2='158' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='217' y1='78' x2='109' y2='158' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='217' y1='78' x2='83' y2='78' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='191' y1='158' x2='109' y2='158' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='191' y1='158' x2='83' y2='78' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='109' y1='158' x2='83' y2='78' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='150' cy='30' r='9' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='217' cy='78' r='9' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='191' cy='158' r='9' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='109' cy='158' r='9' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='83' cy='78' r='9' fill='#1F5FA8'/><text x='150' y='185' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>5 people = 5(4)/2 = 10 channels</text></svg>
figure.caption: Every line is a channel; 5 people already means 10.

tip: If a question adds people, recompute both n values — the jump is often the trap.
